Why I Gave Up Evernote for Notion
Notion is clean, capable, and good enough that I dropped Evernote after almost five years. Quick post on what made me switch.
Evernote and Notion both live in the "cloud notes" category — capture full web pages, support cross-platform (mobile, Windows, Mac). Evernote was genuinely great for me in the early days.
But once Evernote pivoted to paid tiers, it started feeling restrictive. My personal pain points:

I'm a developer — work laptop, home laptop, phone. With Evernote's free-tier 2-device limit, every laptop swap meant deauthorizing the old one. And the deauth itself was capped at three times per month.

Not a huge deal most months, but I did blow through it once when I happened to upload three or four ~10MB files in a row — all of which I wanted attached to specific notes. So either keep them in Evernote and hit the cap, or stash them elsewhere and lose the tight coupling. Ended up moving most files out.

Sometimes I'd upload a bunch of images into a single note and bump into this. And the per-note limit interacts badly with the monthly cap — three notes hitting 25MB and you've already used your monthly quota.
I'm a developer. Markdown wasn't huge when I first picked Evernote, but in the years since it's become standard everywhere. Evernote stuck with the old Word-style editor and kept
about Markdown support without shipping it.
(I recall hearing that Evernote in one specific region got Markdown, but the international version hadn't.)
Workaround for Evernote + Markdown if you need it:
Also — Evernote's code block support is clunky and slow, which started pushing me to look elsewhere.
Evernote's last "stable" release I can find references to was around 2017/09 (
That's the rundown of why I drifted away from Evernote.
Evernote is still good for some workflows — it just stopped matching mine.
Onto Notion — the point of this post.
Beyond Markdown, Notion's UI is clean and minimal — fans of minimalism will love it.
Templates are great too — covered below.
Notion's main features at a glance:
Import from other notes apps (e.g., Evernote — with caveats on formatting)
Walking through these one by one.

. Text, image, heading, list — everything's a block. You can nest blocks (including whole pages) inside other blocks:

Gallery is particularly handy as a task management surface.
Image, video, web embed, audio — exactly what you'd expect.

I don't use these much. Notable — math equations are in here.
That's most of the common blocks. Notion has a lot — best to just sign up and play.
Notion has a huge template gallery. Click "Template" in the sidebar:
Pick what you want, click "Use This Template", and it adds to your sidebar. I personally only use the Notes & Drafts template.
You can also upload your own templates to share.
In Notion, each note is a workspace, and you can create unlimited workspaces. Important caveat —
. Paying for workspace A doesn't apply to workspace B.
For teams this is great — separate workspaces for work and personal, custom URLs per workspace, guest invites.
Notion used to limit free accounts to 1,000 blocks total. Later they removed that limit.
Now the main differences between free and Personal:
If you're a teacher or student with a school email, you can upgrade to Personal for free — ignoring all of those limits:

The upgrade flow — click "Settings & Members" in the sidebar → "Upgrade" → scroll to "Students & educators" → click "Get free Education plan" and verify with your school email. After verification, your workspace upgrades to Personal automatically.
So I'd recommend signing up with a school email from the start if you have one.
(Side note — basically any workspace can be upgraded with an education account.)
I covered Evernote's annoyances, so here are Notion's:
No native interface localization (depends on browser translate)
Lots of features → steeper learning curve
Per-workspace pricing — multiple workspaces multiply your cost. Evernote was per-account flat pricing.

Re: 2FA — Notion's login flow uses email magic codes. If you sign in via Google, lock down your Google account with 2FA so your Notion is protected too. Notion does let you set a password as a fallback, but if you set one, the password login bypasses your Google 2FA. So I'd recommend

If you already set one and can't find the "Remove Password" option, the workaround is — create a new workspace, then remove the password there. Once a workspace has been upgraded, the password can't be removed; new not-yet-upgraded workspaces can remove it.
To be clear — Evernote isn't bad, it just stopped fitting my workflow.
Quick tip — Notion doesn't have a localized interface, but you can use the browser's built-in translate to render everything in your preferred language.

Notion is a clean, capable note-taking app — clean enough that I dropped Evernote after almost five years on it. This post is about why I made the switch — Evernote's pricing/sync/Markdown gaps versus Notion's blocks, Markdown support, templates, workspaces, and the education plan that gives students Notion Personal for free.
